title: SpaceX Propulsion Engineer
setting: SpaceX Hawthorne HQ & McGregor Test Facility
description: You are a propulsion engineer working on the Raptor engine programme. Your days split between design work at the Hawthorne factory in Los Angeles and test campaigns at the McGregor test facility in Texas. The pace is relentless — SpaceX builds a new Raptor every 48 hours, and each engine gets better than the last. You are part of the team pushing the world's most advanced full-flow staged combustion engine towards higher reliability and lower cost.

schedule.activity: Arrive early. Review overnight test data from McGregor — a Raptor 3 ran a full-duration firing at 300 bar chamber pressure.
schedule.note: SpaceX engineers start early and stay late. The pace is not for everyone.

schedule.activity: Morning standup with the turbopump team. Discuss a bearing wear pattern seen in post-test inspection. Decide to change the bearing preload on the next build.
Deep work session: run CEA calculations for a modified injector element that might improve combustion stability at the new higher chamber pressure.
schedule.note: Combustion instability is the hardest problem in rocket engine design.

schedule.activity: Design review meeting for the next Raptor iteration. Present your injector redesign with CFD results showing improved mixing. Get challenged on thermal margins.
Lunch at the SpaceX cafeteria. Casual conversation with a GNC engineer about landing engine requirements for the next Starship flight.
Walk the factory floor. Check the engine currently on the assembly stand. Discuss a weld quality issue with the manufacturing team — the fix adds 2 hours per engine but improves reliability.
schedule.note: SpaceX engineers are expected to be on the factory floor regularly, not just in offices.

schedule.activity: Analyse hot-fire test data for combustion stability. Run FFT on chamber pressure measurements looking for instability modes. Compare with prediction model.
Write test request for next McGregor firing. Define test conditions, instrumentation requirements, and abort criteria.
Late session: review pull requests from junior engineers. Provide feedback on a propellant feed system simulation.
schedule.note: Mentoring is expected. The team is young and growing fast.

schedule.activity: Head home. Check Slack on the drive for any urgent McGregor test results.

responsibilities: Design and analyse rocket engine components (injectors, turbopumps, combustion chambers)
Plan and review hot-fire test campaigns
Analyse test data and drive design improvements
Support engine production and quality issues on the factory floor
Collaborate with systems engineering on vehicle-level integration
Mentor junior engineers and review their work
challenges: The pace is brutal — 60-80 hour weeks during critical test campaigns
Failure is public — when a Starship explodes, the world watches
Combustion instability can appear without warning and is extremely difficult to debug
Balancing innovation speed with reliability requirements
Living between Hawthorne and McGregor for test campaigns
rewards: Working on the most advanced rocket engine ever built
Seeing your designs fly — literally watching your engine power Starship
Unprecedented pace of learning — more engine tests per month than most companies do per year
Being part of the team making humanity multiplanetary
World-class engineering colleagues who push you to be better
